3: The Latin American context is characterized by the lack of basic sanitary infrastructure and the inappropriate management of almost all waste types (Curi et al., 2017). In Argentina as many other developing countries (i) no waste treatment systems are designed yet (Ontiveros and Campanella, 2013; Vicentin et al., 2019); (ii) economic limitations are the main reason of high socio-environmental pressure; (iii) the potential of organic wastes as energy and nutrient source is not taken into account in common methods implemented; (iv) inefficient and fossil (non-re- newable) energy sources are commonly used to transport wastes from one site to another for final deposition; (v) the economy is strongly based in agricultural, livestock and industrial activities operating next to large

urban centres. Although solid waste is an issue to be managed rather than a resource to be energy recovered, recently the growing concern of all those aspects has led to declare its integral treatment and recovery as a strategic theme in the research and development plan "Innovative Argentina 2020", and thus, sanitation legal requirements are expected to be more demanding in the near future. A specific area of interest, is the north-east region of Santa Fe Province where animal, fruits and vegetable primary production and extensive and intensive breeding are important contributors to the generation of organic agricultural waste. Particular attention must be paid to poultry litter (PL), the main residue from the chicken production cycle. It is a heterogeneous mixture, characterized by a sum of the ma- terial used as bedding in association with the animal waste, dead skin,

feed scraps, water, feathers and the resulting microbiota. It also contains antimicrobial and antibiotic residues, which are used as growth pro- moters and for treatment of infections, endocrine disruptors such as chicken metabolic products, and residues of pesticides and herbicides used in cultivation of the grains used for feed manufacture (Santos Dalólio et al., 2017).

On the other hand, due to its contents of organic matter (~675g/kg total carbon nitrogen 1g/kg), phosphorus (~47 g/kg), other cations and its attributes which improve both the physical and chemical soil properties, PL has an enormous recovery potential as organic fertilizer. However, pollution problems occur when raw PL is applied with no previous treatment, causing odour and nutrient loss due to environmental factors such as soil erosion or surface runoff during rainfall since these pollutants may finish in water bodies, affecting soil and water qualities. Thus, several studies suggest that its land applica- tion requires a stabilization step to avoid problems when indiscrimin- ately disposed of in the environment (Pugazhendhi et al., 2020) (Santos Dalólio et al., 2017) (Wohde et al., 2016) (Sanchuki et al., 2011) (Sharpley et al., 2004).

Composting techniques have been widely implemented as low cost and safe alternatives to avoid organic wastes improper disposal leaving the residue exposed to weather conditions and subsequent release of dissolved and particulate pollutants to the environment (Pan et al., 2015) (Awasthi et al., 2020). In the same vein, vermicomposting brings further benefits by combining the action of earthworms and microor- ganisms, through which a better stabilized final product is obtained (Nigussie et al., 2016). Although it was implemented since the ancient Egypt, its introduction to the scientific field as formal treatment tech- nique and its adaptation to organic wastes began in the 1970s in the United States. Since then, a large number of research and projects have been developed and widely implemented in countries such as Cuba, India or Australia (Daicy de Jesús, 2010).

The combination of composting and vermicomposting can be considered as a way of achieving stabilized substrates. Composting en- ables the sanitization of the waste and decompose and stabilize the organic material (Ndegwa and Thompson, 2001) and vermicomposting reduces particle size and encourage the retention of nitrogen and gradual release of phosphorus, along with a reduction in electrical conductivity. In addition to this, vermicompost stimulate higher soil microbial activity than composts (Fornes et al., 2013; Tognetti et al., 2005) and Lazcano et al. (2008) showed that the combined treatment was the most effective in terms of stabilization of the cattle manure.

For all the above mentioned, composting and vermicomposting could be a promising option for PL management within the area of in- terest, mechanisms of both processes in producing bio-fertilizer from the waste show that they are meeting the cleaner production concept. Moreover, the driving force behind this is the global recognition of the necessity to recover useful organic materials and return them to the soil al., 2016).

However, it is important to assess through indexes or numerical values the potential and identify weak aspects of a product, system or service has throughout its life cycle, to make a more complete evalua- tion. Currently, there are many available tools more or less complex, nonetheless, Balkema et al. (2002) reviewed an extensive literature to provide an overview of the methods and indicators currently used. They point out that the Life cycle assessment (LCA) is the method that would provide the most global view of the problem to be analysed, since the others only evaluate one variable at a time: energy, money, environ- mental impacts.

LCA is a powerful methodological tool for quantifying, evaluating, comparing and estimating the environmental aspects attributable to the life cycle of a production system or service through all the stages of its existence (Morero et al., 2017). There are several previous studies which examined different agricultural organic solid waste management sys- tems as a screening tool in a first stage of assessment. Composting and vermicomposting from different feedstock have been assessed from

environmental and energy perspectives, with special attention on global warming and eutrophication potential (Komakech et al., 2016; Li et al., 2018; Rehl and Müller, 2011; Gassara et al., 2011). LCA studies also concluded that composting is having lesser environmental impacts as compared to other organic waste disposals, such as landfilling and incineration (Saer et al., 2013).

The goal of this study is to compare from an energetic, economic and environmental point of view, the two-stage composting vermicompost- ing treatment of two different mixtures of typical wastes in Santa Fe (Argentina) that are currently dumped and spread as fertilizers with no treatment. The work is focused on the potential two-stage composting vermicomposting technique for transforming PL and other organic waste into bio-fertilizer as a sustainable waste management strategy for the near future of the region, with an additional advantage that the treat- ment has been tested on different mixtures at lab-scale. In this sense, the study will be able to provide a more real, novel and global solution for the local problem which will be beneficial for the decision-makers to face these regional issues.

7: The Latin American context is characterized by the lack of basic sanitary infrastructure and the inappropriate management of almost all waste types (Curi et al., 2017). In Argentina as many other developing countries (i) no waste treatment systems are designed yet (Ontiveros and Campanella, 2013; Vicentin et al., 2019); (ii) economic limitations are the main reason of high socio-environmental pressure; (iii) the potential of organic wastes as energy and nutrient source is not taken into account in common methods implemented; (iv) inefficient and fossil (non-re- newable) energy sources are commonly used to transport wastes from one site to another for final deposition; (v) the economy is strongly based in agricultural, livestock and industrial activities operating next to large

urban centres. Although solid waste is an issue to be managed rather than a resource to be energy recovered, recently the growing concern of all those aspects has led to declare its integral treatment and recovery as a strategic theme in the research and development plan "Innovative Argentina 2020", and thus, sanitation legal requirements are expected to be more demanding in the near future. A specific area of interest, is the north-east region of Santa Fe Province where animal, fruits and vegetable primary production and extensive and intensive breeding are important contributors to the generation of organic agricultural waste. Particular attention must be paid to poultry litter (PL), the main residue from the chicken production cycle. It is a heterogeneous mixture, characterized by a sum of the ma- terial used as bedding in association with the animal waste, dead skin,

feed scraps, water, feathers and the resulting microbiota. It also contains antimicrobial and antibiotic residues, which are used as growth pro- moters and for treatment of infections, endocrine disruptors such as chicken metabolic products, and residues of pesticides and herbicides used in cultivation of the grains used for feed manufacture (Santos Dalólio et al., 2017).

On the other hand, due to its contents of organic matter (~675g/kg total carbon nitrogen 1g/kg), phosphorus (~47 g/kg), other cations and its attributes which improve both the physical and chemical soil properties, PL has an enormous recovery potential as organic fertilizer. However, pollution problems occur when raw PL is applied with no previous treatment, causing odour and nutrient loss due to environmental factors such as soil erosion or surface runoff during rainfall since these pollutants may finish in water bodies, affecting soil and water qualities. Thus, several studies suggest that its land applica- tion requires a stabilization step to avoid problems when indiscrimin- ately disposed of in the environment (Pugazhendhi et al., 2020) (Santos Dalólio et al., 2017) (Wohde et al., 2016) (Sanchuki et al., 2011) (Sharpley et al., 2004).

Composting techniques have been widely implemented as low cost and safe alternatives to avoid organic wastes improper disposal leaving the residue exposed to weather conditions and subsequent release of dissolved and particulate pollutants to the environment (Pan et al., 2015) (Awasthi et al., 2020). In the same vein, vermicomposting brings further benefits by combining the action of earthworms and microor- ganisms, through which a better stabilized final product is obtained (Nigussie et al., 2016). Although it was implemented since the ancient Egypt, its introduction to the scientific field as formal treatment tech- nique and its adaptation to organic wastes began in the 1970s in the United States. Since then, a large number of research and projects have been developed and widely implemented in countries such as Cuba, India or Australia (Daicy de Jesús, 2010).

The combination of composting and vermicomposting can be considered as a way of achieving stabilized substrates. Composting en- ables the sanitization of the waste and decompose and stabilize the organic material (Ndegwa and Thompson, 2001) and vermicomposting reduces particle size and encourage the retention of nitrogen and gradual release of phosphorus, along with a reduction in electrical conductivity. In addition to this, vermicompost stimulate higher soil microbial activity than composts (Fornes et al., 2013; Tognetti et al., 2005) and Lazcano et al. (2008) showed that the combined treatment was the most effective in terms of stabilization of the cattle manure.

For all the above mentioned, composting and vermicomposting could be a promising option for PL management within the area of in- terest, mechanisms of both processes in producing bio-fertilizer from the waste show that they are meeting the cleaner production concept. Moreover, the driving force behind this is the global recognition of the necessity to recover useful organic materials and return them to the soil al., 2016).

However, it is important to assess through indexes or numerical values the potential and identify weak aspects of a product, system or service has throughout its life cycle, to make a more complete evalua- tion. Currently, there are many available tools more or less complex, nonetheless, Balkema et al. (2002) reviewed an extensive literature to provide an overview of the methods and indicators currently used. They point out that the Life cycle assessment (LCA) is the method that would provide the most global view of the problem to be analysed, since the others only evaluate one variable at a time: energy, money, environ- mental impacts.

LCA is a powerful methodological tool for quantifying, evaluating, comparing and estimating the environmental aspects attributable to the life cycle of a production system or service through all the stages of its existence (Morero et al., 2017). There are several previous studies which examined different agricultural organic solid waste management sys- tems as a screening tool in a first stage of assessment. Composting and vermicomposting from different feedstock have been assessed from

environmental and energy perspectives, with special attention on global warming and eutrophication potential (Komakech et al., 2016; Li et al., 2018; Rehl and Müller, 2011; Gassara et al., 2011). LCA studies also concluded that composting is having lesser environmental impacts as compared to other organic waste disposals, such as landfilling and incineration (Saer et al., 2013).

The goal of this study is to compare from an energetic, economic and environmental point of view, the two-stage composting vermicompost- ing treatment of two different mixtures of typical wastes in Santa Fe (Argentina) that are currently dumped and spread as fertilizers with no treatment. The work is focused on the potential two-stage composting vermicomposting technique for transforming PL and other organic waste into bio-fertilizer as a sustainable waste management strategy for the near future of the region, with an additional advantage that the treat- ment has been tested on different mixtures at lab-scale. In this sense, the study will be able to provide a more real, novel and global solution for the local problem which will be beneficial for the decision-makers to face these regional issues.

10: Scenarios were designed to treat two different mixtures of organic wastes: Eucalyptus sp. sawdust (ES), rice hulls (RH) and PL. The main substrate (PL) was considered in two different proportions for com- posting process: M1-1RH:3 PL for scenario 1 and M2-0.5RH:1 ES:2 for scenario 2. Each composted mixture, after the 120 days was combined with fresh soil (S) (30%), given two different vermicompost treatments (90 days): T1 = M1+S and T2 = M2+S. M1, M2, T1 and T2 correspond to mixtures and treatments studied previously at laboratory scale (Masin et al., 2020). Published results were used to build the flow diagram of the scenarios studied in Fig. 1 where organic wastes considered are transported by a truck for processing in a two-stage composting vermi- composting plant (2CVP). The material is mixed and dumped in piles or windrows to initialize the 120 days-composting treatment; additional 90 days of vermicomposting were considered to produce bio-fertilizer that is transported by truck to a near farm for land application.

As it can be seen, in Fig. 1 black lines show the mass flow diagram of (a) scenario 1 and (b) scenario 2; green lines indicate biological agents used during vermicomposting: earthworms inlet; and energy re- quirements are pointed out according to the source: red arrows to downstream fossil fuel consumption during transport and the com- posting process itself; and yellow ones to electrical energy demand during conditioning of the substrates prior to the aerobic stabilization proposed and irrigation to maintain an appropriate moisture content, as main electricity consumers considered. Finally, dotted lines show the limits of the mass and energy balance used for the environmental life cycling assessment and the economic analysis described in the following sections.

Finally, interesting conclusions can be obtained from comparisons between the proposed treatments (scenarios 1 and 2 with mass recovery) versus the current regulated practice of final deposition of the material in a landfill. Further conclusions can be made considering a base line that represents the real situation where the raw material is transported and spread over the field as bio-fertilizer.

12: The LCA was conducted according to ISO 14040-44 (ISO, 2006; ISO 14044, 2006) in four phases: the goal and scope definition phase, the inventory analysis phase, the impact assessment phase and the inter- pretation phase. It was implemented to further investigate a sub-system previously studied at laboratory scale to give a border framework to the waste treatment problem, and to highlight the benefits and constraints of its recovery from an environmental point of view.
The LCA performed also sought to identify processes contributing most to the environmental impacts associated with a particular practice,
Fig. 1. Studied scenarios for mass, energy and economic evaluation. a) Scenario 1; b) Scenario 2. Abbreviations: ES, sawdust; RH rice hulls; PL, poultry litter; S, soil.
so that possible improvements in the life cycle of the different technol- logies could be suggested.
13: The objective and scope of the LCA were to assess the environmental impacts of two scenarios treating different waste mixtures by two-stage composting vermicomposting processes. Since two different mixtures of typical wastes in Santa Fe (Argentina) were previously studied at labo- ratory scale, both proposed to recover mass as bio-fertilizers, two sce- narios were compared in terms of the environmental impacts.

Each scenario includes substrates conditioning and transport, stabi- lization by two -stage composting vermicomposting treatment, bio- fertilizer transport to a field for land application, considering avoided products. Avoided products refers specifically to urea fertilizer which is avoided since the treated material, or untreated in the base line scenario, is spread as bio-fertilizer replacing chemical fertilization.
14: The functional unit expresses the function of the system in quanti- tative terms and provides the reference to which all the inputs and outputs of the product system are calculated (ISO, 2006). The functional unit selected is the total waste (PL) annually treated in the 2CVP to produce bio-fertilizer for near eucalyptus plantations. The average amount generated per bird in a 42 day production cycle considered for this study, was 3.6 kg of litter/bird since the measured data available in literature is quite variable within the range from 1.5 to 5.7/kg of lit- ter/bird according to Santos Dalólio et al. (2017). Thus, the total waste annually generated within 6 cycles per year (an appropriate average according to farmers) was considered for the present. The main substrate i.e. the 460 t of PL is considered in two different mixtures: M1-1RH:31 for scenario 1 and M2-0.5RH:1 ES:2 PL for scenario 2.
15: Fig. 1 outlines the main flows within the studied alternatives of treatment. Dotted lines indicate the study boundaries that were taken into account during the inventory.
16: Annual inventory of two alternatives here proposed can be seen in Table 1. Second and third column contains total inputs and outputs per

functional unit, i.e. the annual 460 of PL treatment within two different mixtures previously studied at laboratory scale (Masin et al., 2020). As can be seen, the proposed treatment resulted in organic/inorganic matter changes and organic fertilizer or bio-fertilizer as main product. Avoided products and emissions by its application were also taken into account. Further details for calculations are described in the following paragraphs.

Two stage composting and vermicomposting experiments of indi- vidual waste organic materials, namely ES, RH and PL in 3 different mixtures were studied (Masin et al., 2020). Results published of two out of those three mixtures previously studied, were used to build the al- ternatives for the present work: M1 and M2 After 120 composting days, both mixtures were combined with fresh soil (S) (30%) to improve the performance of earthworms. Twenty clitellated Eisenia fetida earth- worms (mean body weight 0.26 + 0.02 g) were added per kilogram of mixture. The vermicomposting was carried out for 90 days at room temperature (24 + 3 °C) and constant light. Moisture was also kept constant at 60-70%.

As it was above mentioned within functional unit section, it is assumed that 460 of PL are generated during one-year period (to which all inputs and outputs are referred to), nevertheless, given its variable distribution during the year, every 42 days 76.7 t of PL is generated but with dead times between production cycles. Since the treatment of every batch lasts 210 days, there are uncertainties regarding the total time required for the treatment of that total annual amount generated. The proposed 2CVP treating those six batch generated per year will be considered as a first approach of the problem to be solved. Future works should take into account that generation changes during the year and composting-vermicomposting processes are highly dependent on weather conditions. For more specific process design, further details of a particular farm will be required. A low cost system of piles and beds during composting and vermi- composting stage, respectively, was proposed. Receipt material is mixed consuming energy, for calculations a dumper self-loading Piquersa D- 1500-AC 4 X 4, 15.2 HP was taken into account. Periodic turnings allow aeration during composting phase: every 7 days, 17 during 120 days were taken into account. On the other hand, low depth beds and earthworms action during vermicomposting makes aeration require- ment negligible, furthermore, mechanical aeration may affect to earth- worms negatively. For diesel calculations during turnings, an efficiency

of turner was taken from the literature. According to (Burg et al., 2014) it varies between 227 and 255 m³ in relation to pile composition. The total annual volume (V) is known (considering poured bulk density of poultry litter of 0.5 g ml-¹ approximately), and from the pyramid volume: V = 1/3 h x b x 1, where, V is the pile volume, h is its height set as 1 m, b is the base set as 3.5 and 1 is the length; thus total linear meters for the truck to be travelled can be calculated as 1 = 3V/hb. Setting 6 m for piles separation and 10 m length, number of piles and their indi- vidual volume are easily obtained. Considering an efficiency of turner of 255 m³ h-1, the overall time is estimated and with a truck efficiency of 9.88 L/h according to the equipment catalogue. Additionally, irrigation

was made after turnings to maintain an 80% of moisture content, to estimate its energy consumption, the technical characteristics of the appropriate equipment was considered: an electric pump Lowara 7013, 0.37 HP. Finally, since a controlled process was proposed, the volume of leachate was considered negligible and its chemical composition was not determined nor included in the study.

For diesel consumption during feedstock and fertilizer land trans- port, data obtained from Simapro® and the Ecoinvent V.3 database for transport in a freight lorry. It was assumed a distance of 15, 10 and 5 km for ES, RH and PL, respectively from their source to the 2CVP; and 15 km from the last to the farming for land application of the bio-fertilizer. Double distance is accounted for the round-trip path considered.

During this stockpile phase, emissions of CH4, NH3 and N2O were taken into account according to emission factors and the methodology (see supplementary material) published by Peek et al. (2017) and later used to estimate the air emissions from organic waste composted at industrial composting facilities in the Netherlands (RIVM, 2018). Furthermore, CO2 emissions were estimated from total organic carbon removed during composting processes. According to (Beck-Friis et al., 2001) and the carbon determination compiled in a balance sheet showed that the cumulative carbon emissions were in agreement with total losses of carbon from the compost materials. Thus, since TC total removal was 37.6% and 38.5% in scenario 1 and 2, respectively; CO2 emissions during composting phase were easily estimated.

In addition, vermicomposting air emissions were estimated accord- ing to published results for CH4, and N2O (Hobson et al., 2005); and for CO2 according to (Elvira et al., 1998) who establishes that similarly to composting process, CO2 emission can be estimated based on organic matter losses.

Direct and indirect N2O emissions from the use of compost (and untreated poultry litter) as bio-fertilizer were calculated according to the calculation method included as supplementary material presented by the IPCC (2006). Furthermore, NO and NH3 emissions from the use of compost were calculated as described in the methodology report for agriculture (Vonk et al., 2018).

Finally, assuming an eucaliptus plantation, avoided synthetic fertil- izer (urea) was calculated considering that 50 kg urea per hectare is required in the studied region (Carpineti et al., 1995). Nitrogen supply from organic amendments to crops is commonly expressed in the amendment's Nitrogen Fertiliser Replacement Value (Hijbeek et al., 2018). According to Petersen (2003) about 10% was used to estimate the potential of the compost as bio-fertilizer, although, no specific data is available, some works have recognised the low value for composts from different substrates (Kokkora, 2007). Having estimated avoided urea, direct and indirect N20, CO2, NO and NH3 avoided emissions from inorganic N-fertilizers were also calculated as recommended by litera- ture with the same methods above mentioned.

Table 1 also includes two extra alternatives analysed: the landfill and the base line scenarios. The inventory of the landfill is based on Simapro® and the Ecoinvent V.3 database for landfill of biodegradable waste EU-27 with surface and basic sealing meeting european limits for emissions. Site includes landfill gas treatment, leachate treatment, sludge treatment and deposition. The data set covers all relevant process steps/technologies over the supply chain of the represented end-of-life inventory with a good overall data quality (Weidema et al., 2013).
17: Annual inventory of two alternatives here proposed can be seen in Table 1. Second and third column contains total inputs and outputs per

functional unit, i.e. the annual 460 of PL treatment within two different mixtures previously studied at laboratory scale (Masin et al., 2020). As can be seen, the proposed treatment resulted in organic/inorganic matter changes and organic fertilizer or bio-fertilizer as main product. Avoided products and emissions by its application were also taken into account. Further details for calculations are described in the following paragraphs.

Two stage composting and vermicomposting experiments of indi- vidual waste organic materials, namely ES, RH and PL in 3 different mixtures were studied (Masin et al., 2020). Results published of two out of those three mixtures previously studied, were used to build the al- ternatives for the present work: M1 and M2 After 120 composting days, both mixtures were combined with fresh soil (S) (30%) to improve the performance of earthworms. Twenty clitellated Eisenia fetida earth- worms (mean body weight 0.26 + 0.02 g) were added per kilogram of mixture. The vermicomposting was carried out for 90 days at room temperature (24 + 3 °C) and constant light. Moisture was also kept constant at 60-70%.

As it was above mentioned within functional unit section, it is assumed that 460 of PL are generated during one-year period (to which all inputs and outputs are referred to), nevertheless, given its variable distribution during the year, every 42 days 76.7 t of PL is generated but with dead times between production cycles. Since the treatment of every batch lasts 210 days, there are uncertainties regarding the total time required for the treatment of that total annual amount generated. The proposed 2CVP treating those six batch generated per year will be considered as a first approach of the problem to be solved. Future works should take into account that generation changes during the year and composting-vermicomposting processes are highly dependent on weather conditions. For more specific process design, further details of a particular farm will be required. A low cost system of piles and beds during composting and vermi- composting stage, respectively, was proposed. Receipt material is mixed consuming energy, for calculations a dumper self-loading Piquersa D- 1500-AC 4 X 4, 15.2 HP was taken into account. Periodic turnings allow aeration during composting phase: every 7 days, 17 during 120 days were taken into account. On the other hand, low depth beds and earthworms action during vermicomposting makes aeration require- ment negligible, furthermore, mechanical aeration may affect to earth- worms negatively. of turner was taken from the literature. According to (Burg et al., 2014) it varies between 227 and 255 m³ in relation to pile composition. Nitrogen supply from organic amendments to crops is commonly expressed in the amendment's Nitrogen Fertiliser Replacement Value (Hijbeek et al., 2018). According to Petersen (2003) about 10% was used to estimate the potential of the compost as bio-fertilizer, although, no specific data is available, some works have recognised the low value for composts from different substrates (Kokkora, 2007). Having estimated avoided urea, direct and indirect N20, CO2, NO and NH3 avoided emissions from inorganic N-fertilizers were also calculated as recommended by litera- ture with the same methods above mentioned.

Table 1 also includes two extra alternatives analysed: the landfill and the base line scenarios. The inventory of the landfill is based on Simapro® and the Ecoinvent V.3 database for landfill of biodegradable waste EU-27 with surface and basic sealing meeting european limits for emissions. Site includes landfill gas treatment, leachate treatment, sludge treatment and deposition. The data set covers all relevant process steps/technologies over the supply chain of the represented end-of-life inventory with a good overall data quality (Weidema et al., 2013).
20: Annual inventory of two alternatives here proposed can be seen in Table 1. Second and third column contains total inputs and outputs per

functional unit, i.e. the annual 460 of PL treatment within two different mixtures previously studied at laboratory scale (Masin et al., 2020). As can be seen, the proposed treatment resulted in organic/inorganic matter changes and organic fertilizer or bio-fertilizer as main product. Avoided products and emissions by its application were also taken into account. Further details for calculations are described in the following paragraphs.

Two stage composting and vermicomposting experiments of indi- vidual waste organic materials, namely ES, RH and PL in 3 different mixtures were studied (Masin et al., 2020). Results published of two out of those three mixtures previously studied, were used to build the al- ternatives for the present work: M1 and M2 After 120 composting days, both mixtures were combined with fresh soil (S) (30%) to improve the performance of earthworms. Twenty clitellated Eisenia fetida earth- worms (mean body weight 0.26 + 0.02 g) were added per kilogram of mixture. The vermicomposting was carried out for 90 days at room temperature (24 + 3 °C) and constant light. Moisture was also kept constant at 60-70%.

As it was above mentioned within functional unit section, it is assumed that 460 of PL are generated during one-year period (to which all inputs and outputs are referred to), nevertheless, given its variable distribution during the year, every 42 days 76.7 t of PL is generated but with dead times between production cycles. Since the treatment of every batch lasts 210 days, there are uncertainties regarding the total time required for the treatment of that total annual amount generated. The proposed 2CVP treating those six batch generated per year will be considered as a first approach of the problem to be solved. Future works should take into account that generation changes during the year and composting-vermicomposting processes are highly dependent on weather conditions. For more specific process design, further details of a particular farm will be required. A low cost system of piles and beds during composting and vermi- composting stage, respectively, was proposed. Receipt material is mixed consuming energy, for calculations a dumper self-loading Piquersa D- 1500-AC 4 X 4, 15.2 HP was taken into account. Periodic turnings allow aeration during composting phase: every 7 days, 17 during 120 days were taken into account. On the other hand, low depth beds and earthworms action during vermicomposting makes aeration require- ment negligible, furthermore, mechanical aeration may affect to earth- worms negatively. a TC, Total Carbon; TOC, Total Organic Carbon.

of turner was taken from the literature. According to (Burg et al., 2014) it varies between 227 and 255 m³ in relation to pile composition. The total annual volume (V) is known (considering poured bulk density of poultry litter of 0.5 g ml-¹ approximately), and from the pyramid volume: V = 1/3 h x b x 1, where, V is the pile volume, h is its height set as 1 m, b is the base set as 3.5 and 1 is the length; thus total linear meters for the truck to be travelled can be calculated as 1 = 3V/hb. Setting 6 m for piles separation and 10 m length, number of piles and their indi- vidual volume are easily obtained. Considering an efficiency of turner of 255 m³ h-1, the overall time is estimated and with a truck efficiency of 9.88 L/h according to the equipment catalogue. Additionally, irrigation

was made after turnings to maintain an 80% of moisture content, to estimate its energy consumption, the technical characteristics of the appropriate equipment was considered: an electric pump Lowara 7013, 0.37 HP. Finally, since a controlled process was proposed, the volume of leachate was considered negligible and its chemical composition was not determined nor included in the study.

For diesel consumption during feedstock and fertilizer land trans- port, data obtained from Simapro® and the Ecoinvent V.3 database for transport in a freight lorry. It was assumed a distance of 15, 10 and 5 km for ES, RH and PL, respectively from their source to the 2CVP; and 15 km from the last to the farming for land application of the bio-fertilizer. Double distance is accounted for the round-trip path considered.

During this stockpile phase, emissions of CH4, NH3 and N2O were taken into account according to emission factors and the methodology (see supplementary material) published by Peek et al. (2017) and later used to estimate the air emissions from organic waste composted at industrial composting facilities in the Netherlands (RIVM, 2018). Furthermore, CO2 emissions were estimated from total organic carbon removed during composting processes. According to (Beck-Friis et al., 2001) and the carbon determination compiled in a balance sheet showed that the cumulative carbon emissions were in agreement with total losses of carbon from the compost materials. Thus, since TC total removal was 37.6% and 38.5% in scenario 1 and 2, respectively; CO2 emissions during composting phase were easily estimated.

In addition, vermicomposting air emissions were estimated accord- ing to published results for CH4, and N2O (Hobson et al., 2005); and for CO2 according to (Elvira et al., 1998) who establishes that similarly to composting process, CO2 emission can be estimated based on organic matter losses.

Direct and indirect N2O emissions from the use of compost (and untreated poultry litter) as bio-fertilizer were calculated according to the calculation method included as supplementary material presented by the IPCC (2006). Furthermore, NO and NH3 emissions from the use of compost were calculated as described in the methodology report for agriculture (Vonk et al., 2018).

Finally, assuming an eucaliptus plantation, avoided synthetic fertil- izer (urea) was calculated considering that 50 kg urea per hectare is required in the studied region (Carpineti et al., 1995). Nitrogen supply from organic amendments to crops is commonly expressed in the amendment's Nitrogen Fertiliser Replacement Value (Hijbeek et al., 2018). According to Petersen (2003) about 10% was used to estimate the potential of the compost as bio-fertilizer, although, no specific data is available, some works have recognised the low value for composts from different substrates (Kokkora, 2007). Having estimated avoided urea, direct and indirect N20, CO2, NO and NH3 avoided emissions from inorganic N-fertilizers were also calculated as recommended by litera- ture with the same methods above mentioned.

Site includes landfill gas treatment, leachate treatment, sludge treatment and deposition. The data set covers all relevant process steps/technologies over the supply chain of the represented end-of-life inventory with a good overall data quality (Weidema et al., 2013).

Results published of two out of those three mixtures previously studied, were used to build the al- ternatives for the present work: M1 and M2 After 120 composting days, both mixtures were combined with fresh soil (S) (30%) to improve the performance of earthworms. Twenty clitellated Eisenia fetida earth- worms (mean body weight 0.26 + 0.02 g) were added per kilogram of mixture. The vermicomposting was carried out for 90 days at room temperature (24 + 3 °C) and constant light. Moisture was also kept constant at 60-70%.

As it was above mentioned within functional unit section, it is assumed that 460 of PL are generated during one-year period (to which all inputs and outputs are referred to), nevertheless, given its variable distribution during the year, every 42 days 76.7 t of PL is generated but with dead times between production cycles. Since the treatment of every batch lasts 210 days, there are uncertainties regarding the total time required for the treatment of that total annual amount generated. The proposed 2CVP treating those six batch generated per year will be considered as a first approach of the problem to be solved. Future works should take into account that generation changes during the year and composting-vermicomposting processes are highly dependent on weather conditions. For more specific process design, further details of a particular farm will be required. A low cost system of piles and beds during composting and vermi- composting stage, respectively, was proposed. Receipt material is mixed consuming energy, for calculations a dumper self-loading Piquersa D- 1500-AC 4 X 4, 15.2 HP was taken into account. Periodic turnings allow aeration during composting phase: every 7 days, 17 during 120 days were taken into account. On the other hand, low depth beds and earthworms action during vermicomposting makes aeration require- ment negligible, furthermore, mechanical aeration may affect to earth- worms negatively. a TC, Total Carbon; TOC, Total Organic Carbon.

of turner was taken from the literature. According to (Burg et al., 2014) it varies between 227 and 255 m³ in relation to pile composition. The total annual volume (V) is known (considering poured bulk density of poultry litter of 0.5 g ml-¹ approximately), and from the pyramid volume: V = 1/3 h x b x 1, where, V is the pile volume, h is its height set as 1 m, b is the base set as 3.5 and 1 is the length; thus total linear meters for the truck to be travelled can be calculated as 1 = 3V/hb. Setting 6 m for piles separation and 10 m length, number of piles and their indi- vidual volume are easily obtained. Considering an efficiency of turner of 255 m³ h-1, the overall time is estimated and with a truck efficiency of 9.88 L/h according to the equipment catalogue. Additionally, irrigation

was made after turnings to maintain an 80% of moisture content, to estimate its energy consumption, the technical characteristics of the appropriate equipment was considered: an electric pump Lowara 7013, 0.37 HP. Finally, since a controlled process was proposed, the volume of leachate was considered negligible and its chemical composition was not determined nor included in the study.

For diesel consumption during feedstock and fertilizer land trans- port, data obtained from Simapro® and the Ecoinvent V.3 database for transport in a freight lorry. It was assumed a distance of 15, 10 and 5 km for ES, RH and PL, respectively from their source to the 2CVP; and 15 km from the last to the farming for land application of the bio-fertilizer. Double distance is accounted for the round-trip path considered.

During this stockpile phase, emissions of CH4, NH3 and N2O were taken into account according to emission factors and the methodology (see supplementary material) published by Peek et al. (2017) and later used to estimate the air emissions from organic waste composted at industrial composting facilities in the Netherlands (RIVM, 2018). Furthermore, CO2 emissions were estimated from total organic carbon removed during composting processes. According to (Beck-Friis et al., 2001) and the carbon determination compiled in a balance sheet showed that the cumulative carbon emissions were in agreement with total losses of carbon from the compost materials. Thus, since TC total removal was 37.6% and 38.5% in scenario 1 and 2, respectively; CO2 emissions during composting phase were easily estimated.

In addition, vermicomposting air emissions were estimated accord- ing to published results for CH4, and N2O (Hobson et al., 2005); and for CO2 according to (Elvira et al., 1998) who establishes that similarly to composting process, CO2 emission can be estimated based on organic matter losses.

Direct and indirect N2O emissions from the use of compost (and untreated poultry litter) as bio-fertilizer were calculated according to the calculation method included as supplementary material presented by the IPCC (2006). Furthermore, NO and NH3 emissions from the use of compost were calculated as described in the methodology report for agriculture (Vonk et al., 2018).

Finally, assuming an eucaliptus plantation, avoided synthetic fertil- izer (urea) was calculated considering that 50 kg urea per hectare is required in the studied region (Carpineti et al., 1995). Nitrogen supply from organic amendments to crops is commonly expressed in the amendment's Nitrogen Fertiliser Replacement Value (Hijbeek et al., 2018). According to Petersen (2003) about 10% was used to estimate the potential of the compost as bio-fertilizer, although, no specific data is available, some works have recognised the low value for composts from different substrates (Kokkora, 2007). Having estimated avoided urea, direct and indirect N20, CO2, NO and NH3 avoided emissions from inorganic N-fertilizers were also calculated as recommended by litera- ture with the same methods above mentioned.

Table 1 also includes two extra alternatives analysed: the landfill and the base line scenarios. The inventory of the landfill is based on Simapro® and the Ecoinvent V.3 database for landfill of biodegradable waste EU-27 with surface and basic sealing meeting european limits for emissions. Site includes landfill gas treatment, leachate treatment, sludge treatment and deposition. The data set covers all relevant process steps/technologies over the supply chain of the represented end-of-life inventory with a good overall data quality (Weidema et al., 2013).

functional unit, i.e. the annual 460 of PL treatment within two different mixtures previously studied at laboratory scale (Masin et al., 2020). a TC, Total Carbon; TOC, Total Organic Carbon.

of turner was taken from the literature. According to (Burg et al., 2014) it varies between 227 and 255 m³ in relation to pile composition. The total annual volume (V) is known (considering poured bulk density of poultry litter of 0.5 g ml-¹ approximately), and from the pyramid volume: V = 1/3 h x b x 1, where, V is the pile volume, h is its height set as 1 m, b is the base set as 3.5 and 1 is the length; thus total linear meters for the truck to be travelled can be calculated as 1 = 3V/hb. Setting 6 m for piles separation and 10 m length, number of piles and their indi- vidual volume are easily obtained. Considering an efficiency of turner of 255 m³ h-1, the overall time is estimated and with a truck efficiency of 9.88 L/h according to the equipment catalogue. Additionally, irrigation

was made after turnings to maintain an 80% of moisture content, to estimate its energy consumption, the technical characteristics of the appropriate equipment was considered: an electric pump Lowara 7013, 0.37 HP. Finally, since a controlled process was proposed, the volume of leachate was considered negligible and its chemical composition was not determined nor included in the study.

For diesel consumption during feedstock and fertilizer land trans- port, data obtained from Simapro® and the Ecoinvent V.3 database for transport in a freight lorry. It was assumed a distance of 15, 10 and 5 km for ES, RH and PL, respectively from their source to the 2CVP; and 15 km from the last to the farming for land application of the bio-fertilizer. Double distance is accounted for the round-trip path considered.

During this stockpile phase, emissions of CH4, NH3 and N2O were taken into account according to emission factors and the methodology (see supplementary material) published by Peek et al. (2017) and later used to estimate the air emissions from organic waste composted at industrial composting facilities in the Netherlands (RIVM, 2018). Furthermore, CO2 emissions were estimated from total organic carbon removed during composting processes. According to (Beck-Friis et al., 2001) and the carbon determination compiled in a balance sheet showed that the cumulative carbon emissions were in agreement with total losses of carbon from the compost materials. Thus, since TC total removal was 37.6% and 38.5% in scenario 1 and 2, respectively; CO2 emissions during composting phase were easily estimated.

In addition, vermicomposting air emissions were estimated accord- ing to published results for CH4, and N2O (Hobson et al., 2005); and for CO2 according to (Elvira et al., 1998) who establishes that similarly to composting process, CO2 emission can be estimated based on organic matter losses.

Direct and indirect N2O emissions from the use of compost (and untreated poultry litter) as bio-fertilizer were calculated according to the calculation method included as supplementary material presented by the IPCC (2006). Furthermore, NO and NH3 emissions from the use of compost were calculated as described in the methodology report for agriculture (Vonk et al., 2018).

Finally, assuming an eucaliptus plantation, avoided synthetic fertil- izer (urea) was calculated considering that 50 kg urea per hectare is required in the studied region (Carpineti et al., 1995). Nitrogen supply from organic amendments to crops is commonly expressed in the amendment's Nitrogen Fertiliser Replacement Value (Hijbeek et al., 2018). According to Petersen (2003) about 10% was used to estimate the potential of the compost as bio-fertilizer, although, no specific data is available, some works have recognised the low value for composts from different substrates (Kokkora, 2007). Having estimated avoided urea, direct and indirect N20, CO2, NO and NH3 avoided emissions from inorganic N-fertilizers were also calculated as recommended by litera- ture with the same methods above mentioned.

Table 1 also includes two extra alternatives analysed: the landfill and the base line scenarios. The inventory of the landfill is based on Simapro® and the Ecoinvent V.3 database for landfill of biodegradable waste EU-27 with surface and basic sealing meeting european limits for emissions. Site includes landfill gas treatment, leachate treatment, sludge treatment and deposition. The data set covers all relevant process steps/technologies over the supply chain of the represented end-of-life inventory with a good overall data quality (Weidema et al., 2013).
27: Annual inventory of two alternatives here proposed can be seen in Table 1. Second and third column contains total inputs and outputs per

functional unit, i.e. the annual 460 of PL treatment within two different mixtures previously studied at laboratory scale (Masin et al., 2020). As can be seen, the proposed treatment resulted in organic/inorganic matter changes and organic fertilizer or bio-fertilizer as main product. Avoided products and emissions by its application were also taken into account. Further details for calculations are described in the following paragraphs.

Two stage composting and vermicomposting experiments of indi- vidual waste organic materials, namely ES, RH and PL in 3 different mixtures were studied (Masin et al., 2020). Results published of two out of those three mixtures previously studied, were used to build the al- ternatives for the present work: M1 and M2 After 120 composting days, both mixtures were combined with fresh soil (S) (30%) to improve the performance of earthworms. Twenty clitellated Eisenia fetida earth- worms (mean body weight 0.26 + 0.02 g) were added per kilogram of mixture. The vermicomposting was carried out for 90 days at room temperature (24 + 3 °C) and constant light. Moisture was also kept constant at 60-70%.

As it was above mentioned within functional unit section, it is assumed that 460 of PL are generated during one-year period (to which all inputs and outputs are referred to), nevertheless, given its variable distribution during the year, every 42 days 76.7 t of PL is generated but with dead times between production cycles. Since the treatment of every batch lasts 210 days, there are uncertainties regarding the total time required for the treatment of that total annual amount generated. The proposed 2CVP treating those six batch generated per year will be considered as a first approach of the problem to be solved. Future works should take into account that generation changes during the year and composting-vermicomposting processes are highly dependent on weather conditions. For more specific process design, further details of a particular farm will be required. A low cost system of piles and beds during composting and vermi- composting stage, respectively, was proposed. Receipt material is mixed consuming energy, for calculations a dumper self-loading Piquersa D- 1500-AC 4 X 4, 15.2 HP was taken into account. Periodic turnings allow aeration during composting phase: every 7 days, 17 during 120 days were taken into account. On the other hand, low depth beds and earthworms action during vermicomposting makes aeration require- ment negligible, furthermore, mechanical aeration may affect to earth- worms negatively. 28: The LCA was performed using the SimaPro® V.8 software with Ecoinvent database V.3 (Weidema et al., 2013). The chosen impact assessment methodology was ReCiPe 2016 midpoint method, Hierar- chist version with eighteen impact categories: GW, global warming [kg COeq.]; OD, stratospheric ozone depletion [kg CFC11 eq.]; IR, ionizing radiation [kBq Co-60 eq.]; OFHH, ozone formation human health [kg NO eq.]; PMF, fine particulate matter formation [kg PM2.5 eq.]; OFTE, ozone formation terrestrial ecosystems [kg NO eq.]; TA, terrestrial acidification [kg SO2 eq.]; FE, freshwater eutrophication [kg P eq.]; ME,

marine eutrophication [kg N eq.]; TET, terrestrial ecotoxicity [kg 1.4-DCB]; FET, freshwater ecotoxicity [kg 1.4-DCB]; ME, marine eco- toxicity [kg 1.4-DCB]; HCT, human carcinogenic toxicity [kg 1.4-DCB]; HNCT, human non-carcinogenic toxicity [kg 1.4-DCB]; LU, land use [m²a crop eq.]; MRS, mineral resource scarcity [kg Cu eq.]; FRS, fossil resource scarcity [kg oil eq.]; WC water consumption [m³ This is the default ReCiPe midpoint method and it is a new version of ReCiPe (2008) and it was created by RIVM, Radboud University, Norwegian University of Science and Technology and PRé Consultants (Huijbregts et al., 2017).

In addition to the environmental analysis of the scenarios, the energy balance was performed. Conversion factors for the lower calorific value of diesel was obtained from literature (MEyM, 2017). Thus, total elec- trical energy consumption was 3.50 and 4.24 MWh for scenario 1 and 2, respectively. In terms of diesel, it was 16.05 and 20.84 MWh, respec- tively. While in the landfill, 106 MWh and 41 MWh corresponds to electricity and diesel consumption, respectively. Finally, no electricity is required for no treatment in the base line scenario; nonetheless, 6.01 MWh of diesel is used to transport the untreated bio-fertilizer for land application.

2.2.2. Cumulative energy demand Additionally, an energy analysis was performed based on the cu- mulative energy demand (CED) to investigate the energy use throughout the life cycle. This includes the direct uses as well as the indirect con- sumption of energy due to the use (Hischier et al., 2010).

31: To the economic analysis, the total operating cost was assessed considering the inputs and outputs included in the inventory, and without taking into account the total plant costs, i.e. acquisition costs of equipment, facilities, pipes, process, instrumentation, insulation, buildings and constructions, electrical installation and auxiliary facil- ities; neither indirect ones, included engineering and construction costs, nor fixed capital cost.

For the calculation of emission reduction certificates (CERs), it is important to highlight that its prices widely fluctuate (as reported in 2006 varied between 3.9 and 22 US$/tCO2) and is not closely correlated with traditional macro-economic factors. Its formation is a result of both fixed (i.e., linked to the price of EU Allowance) and variable (e.g., risk profile) components. In Latin America, for example, CER prices have stabilized at around 15.4 US$/tCO2 for less risk-averse sellers and 8.8 US $/tCO2 for more risk-averse sellers (UNEP, 2007). Recent data from the European CO2 negotiation system show a general drastic fall in the market CER's price, from an average of 19.2 US$/tCO2 in 2008 to 0.26 US$/tCO2 in 2018. The end of 2011 is characterized by a sharp decline and it is maintained until nowadays. For calculations, the last registered data in august 2019 was considered, equal to 0.23 US$/tCO2 (IETA, 2019).

The sale price of electricity given by the energy company of the Province of Santa Fe in august 2019, was 13.38 US$/kWh considering an industry that consumes less than 50 kW, i.e a consumption less than or equal to 800 kWh/month. For the diesel, an average price from the National Ministry of Energy and Mining for the corresponding region was considered, 0.778 US$/1 (MEyM, 2017). The sale price of an organic fertilizer obtained from the literature show high variability, to have an idea, a given price from the literature (McFarland, 2000), and updated to 2015 from the US consumer price index, resulting in a value of 79 US $/t. However, in the real life, regional farmers sell the raw material at the price that the buyer is willing to pay, that is between the range 5.36 and 7.14 US$/t. Furthermore, the producer would not be willing to buy

a much more expensive although treated product, thus the revenues in this aspect were calculated as follow, the lower limit for the raw material in the baseline scenario and the upper limit for treated material in the scenario 1 and 2. Both values are far from the ideal or theoretical value from literature.

Finally, savings from avoided urea were calculated according to the annual average price of 175.6 US$/t published by an updated website (IndexMundi, 2019). Nonetheless, the Food and Agriculture Organiza- tion of the United Nations has recently published an average price from 2010 to 2014 of about 350.6 US$/t (FAO, 2015).

The sale price of an organic fertilizer obtained from the literature show high variability, to have an idea, a given price from the literature (McFarland, 2000), and updated to 2015 from the US consumer price index, resulting in a value of 79 US $/t. However, in the real life, regional farmers sell the raw material at the price that the buyer is willing to pay, that is between the range 5.36 and 7.14 US$/t. Furthermore, the producer would not be willing to buy

a much more expensive although treated product, thus the revenues in this aspect were calculated as follow, the lower limit for the raw material in the baseline scenario and the upper limit for treated material in the scenario 1 and 2. Both values are far from the ideal or theoretical value from literature.

Finally, savings from avoided urea were calculated according to the annual average price of 175.6 US$/t published by an updated website (IndexMundi, 2019). Nonetheless, the Food and Agriculture Organiza- tion of the United Nations has recently published an average price from 2010 to 2014 of about 350.6 US$/t (FAO, 2015).
34: To the economic analysis, the total operating cost was assessed considering the inputs and outputs included in the inventory, and without taking into account the total plant costs, i.e. acquisition costs of equipment, facilities, pipes, process, instrumentation, insulation, buildings and constructions, electrical installation and auxiliary facil- ities; neither indirect ones, included engineering and construction costs, nor fixed capital cost.

For the calculation of emission reduction certificates (CERs), it is important to highlight that its prices widely fluctuate (as reported in 2006 varied between 3.9 and 22 US$/tCO2) and is not closely correlated with traditional macro-economic factors. Its formation is a result of both fixed (i.e., linked to the price of EU Allowance) and variable (e.g., risk profile) components. In Latin America, for example, CER prices have stabilized at around 15.4 US$/tCO2 for less risk-averse sellers and 8.8 US $/tCO2 for more risk-averse sellers (UNEP, 2007). Recent data from the European CO2 negotiation system show a general drastic fall in the market CER's price, from an average of 19.2 US$/tCO2 in 2008 to 0.26 US$/tCO2 in 2018. The end of 2011 is characterized by a sharp decline and it is maintained until nowadays. For calculations, the last registered data in august 2019 was considered, equal to 0.23 US$/tCO2 (IETA, 2019).

The sale price of electricity given by the energy company of the Province of Santa Fe in august 2019, was 13.38 US$/kWh considering an industry that consumes less than 50 kW, i.e a consumption less than or equal to 800 kWh/month. For the diesel, an average price from the National Ministry of Energy and Mining for the corresponding region was considered, 0.778 US$/1 (MEyM, 2017). The sale price of an organic fertilizer obtained from the literature show high variability, to have an idea, a given price from the literature (McFarland, 2000), and updated to 2015 from the US consumer price index, resulting in a value of 79 US $/t. However, in the real life, regional farmers sell the raw material at the price that the buyer is willing to pay, that is between the range 5.36 and 7.14 US$/t. Furthermore, the producer would not be willing to buy

a much more expensive although treated product, thus the revenues in this aspect were calculated as follow, the lower limit for the raw material in the baseline scenario and the upper limit for treated material in the scenario 1 and 2. Both values are far from the ideal or theoretical value from literature.

Finally, savings from avoided urea were calculated according to the annual average price of 175.6 US$/t published by an updated website (IndexMundi, 2019). Nonetheless, the Food and Agriculture Organiza- tion of the United Nations has recently published an average price from 2010 to 2014 of about 350.6 US$/t (FAO, 2015).
35: <header>3. Results and discussion </header>
3.1. LCA results

Designed treatment scenarios were based on previous results: two- stage composting vermicomposting yields obtained at lab-scale. Simi- larly, they were based on previous studies, that have confirmed better results from composting-vermicosposting compared to composting processes alone.

In this sense, Lazcano et al. (2008) showed that the combined pro- cesses (composting + vermicomposting) was the most effective in terms of stabilization of the cattle manure. In addition, earthworms stimulated the retention of nitrogen and gradual release of P, as well as a reduction in electrical conductivity, thereby producing improved substrates for agricultural use. Also, some authors suggest that vermicompost stimu- late higher soil microbial activity than composts (Fornes et al., 2012; Tognetti et al., 2005) and that the combination of the processes shortens stabilization time (Ndegwa and Thompson, 2001).

LCA results from Fig. 2 shows the relative contribution of each sce- nario to different environmental impacts. A negative contribution is shown on the positive axis and vice versa. Among 18 categories included by the selected method, only 14 were considered as they are the most relevant in the studied area, the impact on marine environments, land use and water consumption was not taken into account, the two latter for not being scarce resources.

As can be seen in Fig. 2a, negative contributions are due to local air emissions produced by the treatment itself and fossil fuel and electricity consumption. On the other hand, just two positive contributions can be detected for HTC and MRS in scenario 1 and 2 due to the avoided products. Evidently, there is no significant differences between both scenarios. However, when data presented in Fig. 2a is analysed in detail, scenario 2 shows slightly higher negative contributions compared to scenario 1. These results are expected when inputs/outputs of Table 1 are observed. For instance, greater consumption of fossil fuels and en- ergy in scenario 2 versus scenario 1 produces unfailingly grater outputs such as emissions and results in a higher negative environmental contribution.

When a comparison with Fig. 2b is made, can be denoted that the main negative contribution comes from the landfill and the base line scenarios in 11 out of 14 categories. Main negative contributions of scenario 1 and 2 against the landfill are maybe due to that the last corresponds to a sanitary landfill with high quality standards, that provide a safe place for final deposition. Positive contributions of the base line scenario are due to no fossil fuel consumption (except for transportation of the raw material for land application), avoided chemical fertilizer and discharges/emissions related. Nonetheless, the base line scenario has important negative contributions because the material with no treatment is spread over the field and produces emissions.

It is important to remark at this point, although a fixed nitrogen fertilizer replacement value was used to assess the bio-fertilizer and to calculate the chemical fertilizer avoided, the availability of N-com- pounds vary from substrate to substrate, thus scenario 1 and 2 produces a bio-fertilizer that is supposed to be of higher quality against the base line. However, no distinction was made tough, because an approximate

Abbreviations: GW, Global warming; OD, Stratospheric ozone depletion; IR, Ionizing radiation; OFHH, Ozone for- mation Human health; PMF, Fine particulate matter formation; OFTE, Ozone formation Terrestrial ecosystems; TA, Terrestrial acidification; FE, Freshwater eutrophication; TET, Terrestrial ecotoxicity; FET, Freshwater ecotoxicity; HCT, Human carcinogenic toxicity; HNCT, Human non-carcinogenic toxicity; MRS, Mineral resource scarcity; FRS, Fossil resource scarcity; WC, Water consumption.
value was taken from literature.

Any scenario of treatment improves the situation of final deposition in a landfill or spreading of raw material (base line scenario). They present the best environmental performance in 11 out of 14 impact categories analysed, demonstrating that composting/vermicomposting are the lowest energy consumers. Although the environmental assess- ment was restricted to quantifiable inputs and outputs, is important to clarify, that there are many other toxic compounds such antibiotics, endocrine disrupting compounds, etc. that are distributed in the envi- ronment, depending on more specific local conditions, for instance the weather, the soil characteristics, the field slope, the proximity to water bodies, etc. In Argentina, the risk of environmental pollution by inten- sive poultry production is higher because of the farms are generally near river basins and the primary production region is characterized by high rainfall and hilly landscapes that can facilitate the runoff and leaching losses (Curi et al., 2017). Despite the fact that toxic compounds are not assessed, the LCA analysis confirms the necessity of including a treat- ment plant.

Despite the fact that an LCA is one out of many other tools available, it is remarkable its usefulness to perform the comparison of waste management alternatives studied and to visualize the magnitude of each impact in order to identify strategies that may prevent or minimise the negative contributions. This is exactly the main aim of LCA imple- mentation (Laurent et al., 2014). For instance, considering the global warming category, it is evident that while the landfill scenario has a relative negative contribution of around 60%, scenario 1, 2 and the base line contributes with a 11%, 10% and 15%, respectively. As a result, actions designed to reduce distances for land transportation of raw material and products within the treatment scenarios, would have an evident effect of fossil fuel consumption and its influence to the global warming.

Although the results are strictly dependent on system boundaries and local specificities of every particular case, in general terms LCA studies that assesses typical alternatives for waste management (no treatment, home composting, final disposal in landfill, incineration, waste valor- isation by nutrient recovery with composting/vermicomposting strate- gies, anaerobic digestion, etc.), have similar findings: (i) composting and material recovery are good options from an environmental point of view (Erses Yay, 2015a) (Ahamed et al., 2016), they reduces burdens to the environment, agriculture, vegetation, aquatic life and human health (Elwan 2015); (ii) landfilling and incineration are the worst waste final disposal alternatives (Erses Yay, 2015b) (Fernández-Nava et al., 2014) (iii) the second did not show significant benefits over the first (Lima et al., 2018) though it reduces damage in the resources category (Fernandez-Nava et al., 2014) since some energy can be recovered; other studies establishes that integrated/combined management including a mix of recycling, composting, anaerobic digestion and landfill had the
lowest overall environmental impact (Sharma and Chandel, 2017); that the transport has a significant impact in the environment (Fernández-- Nava et al., 2014).

Many authors were involved in anaerobic digestion options, it con- tributes to reducing the impact in all the damage categories (Fernán- dez-Nava et al., 2014) (Ahamed et al., 2016), although its management is critical and methane losses must be kept very small to avoid global warming affection (Komakech et al., 2015). Other authors established that when it is used prior to composting and when minimal distance separates both treatments within the farm, good results are obtained (Li et al., 2018); Arafat et al. (2015), found that anaerobic digestion and gasification had a better environmental performance than incineration, bio-landfills and composting; Liikanen et al. (2018), compared it with incineration in a developing country context, although both systems admits national matrix energy substitution by electricity production, based on their results and other literature studies, they found that anaerobic digestion showed better environmental results and they conclude that incineration is not as favourable in Brazil as in other countries (e.g. China) due to the rather low amount of avoided emissions resulting from electricity substitution, considering that average elec- tricity production in Brazil is dominated by hydropower.

Finally, composting and vermicomposting are widely regarded as a clean and sustainable method to manage organic waste and the scientific community is striving to demonstrate it. However, the environmental impact in terms of emitted greenhouse gases and economic potential of these processes are under discussion, especially because of the wide range in market value for organic fertilizer and differences in cost for the type of composing or vermicomposting system which could affect its economic feasibility (Lim et al., 2016).

For instance, considering the global warming category, it is evident that while the landfill scenario has a relative negative contribution of around 60%, scenario 1, 2 and the base line contributes with a 11%, 10% and 15%, respectively. As a result, actions designed to reduce distances for land transportation of raw material and products within the treatment scenarios, would have an evident effect of fossil fuel consumption and its influence to the global warming.

Although the results are strictly dependent on system boundaries and local specificities of every particular case, in general terms LCA studies that assesses typical alternatives for waste management (no treatment, home composting, final disposal in landfill, incineration, waste valor- isation by nutrient recovery with composting/vermicomposting strate- gies, anaerobic digestion, etc.), have similar findings: (i) composting and material recovery are good options from an environmental point of view (Erses Yay, 2015a) (Ahamed et al., 2016), they reduces burdens to the environment, agriculture, vegetation, aquatic life and human health (Elwan 2015); (ii) landfilling and incineration are the worst waste final disposal alternatives (Erses Yay, 2015b) (Fernández-Nava et al., 2014) (iii) the second did not show significant benefits over the first (Lima et al., 2018) though it reduces damage in the resources category (Fernandez-Nava et al., 2014) since some energy can be recovered; other studies establishes that integrated/combined management including a mix of recycling, composting, anaerobic digestion and landfill had the
lowest overall environmental impact (Sharma and Chandel, 2017); that the transport has a significant impact in the environment (Fernández-- Nava et al., 2014).

Many authors were involved in anaerobic digestion options, it con- tributes to reducing the impact in all the damage categories (Fernán- dez-Nava et al., 2014) (Ahamed et al., 2016), although its management is critical and methane losses must be kept very small to avoid global warming affection (Komakech et al., 2015). Other authors established that when it is used prior to composting and when minimal distance separates both treatments within the farm, good results are obtained (Li et al., 2018); Arafat et al. (2015), found that anaerobic digestion and gasification had a better environmental performance than incineration, bio-landfills and composting; Liikanen et al. (2018), compared it with incineration in a developing country context, although both systems admits national matrix energy substitution by electricity production, based on their results and other literature studies, they found that anaerobic digestion showed better environmental results and they conclude that incineration is not as favourable in Brazil as in other countries (e.g. China) due to the rather low amount of avoided emissions resulting from electricity substitution, considering that average elec- tricity production in Brazil is dominated by hydropower.

Finally, composting and vermicomposting are widely regarded as a clean and sustainable method to manage organic waste and the scientific community is striving to demonstrate it. However, the environmental impact in terms of emitted greenhouse gases and economic potential of these processes are under discussion, especially because of the wide range in market value for organic fertilizer and differences in cost for the type of composing or vermicomposting system which could affect its economic feasibility (Lim et al., 2016).
39: <header>3. Results and discussion </header>
3.1. LCA results

Designed treatment scenarios were based on previous results: two- stage composting vermicomposting yields obtained at lab-scale. Simi- larly, they were based on previous studies, that have confirmed better results from composting-vermicosposting compared to composting processes alone.

In this sense, Lazcano et al. (2008) showed that the combined pro- cesses (composting + vermicomposting) was the most effective in terms of stabilization of the cattle manure. In addition, earthworms stimulated the retention of nitrogen and gradual release of P, as well as a reduction in electrical conductivity, thereby producing improved substrates for agricultural use. Also, some authors suggest that vermicompost stimu- late higher soil microbial activity than composts (Fornes et al., 2012; Tognetti et al., 2005) and that the combination of the processes shortens stabilization time (Ndegwa and Thompson, 2001).

LCA results from Fig. 2 shows the relative contribution of each sce- nario to different environmental impacts. A negative contribution is shown on the positive axis and vice versa. Among 18 categories included by the selected method, only 14 were considered as they are the most relevant in the studied area, the impact on marine environments, land use and water consumption was not taken into account, the two latter for not being scarce resources.

As can be seen in Fig. 2a, negative contributions are due to local air emissions produced by the treatment itself and fossil fuel and electricity consumption. On the other hand, just two positive contributions can be detected for HTC and MRS in scenario 1 and 2 due to the avoided products. Evidently, there is no significant differences between both scenarios. However, when data presented in Fig. 2a is analysed in detail, scenario 2 shows slightly higher negative contributions compared to scenario 1. These results are expected when inputs/outputs of Table 1 are observed. For instance, greater consumption of fossil fuels and en- ergy in scenario 2 versus scenario 1 produces unfailingly grater outputs such as emissions and results in a higher negative environmental contribution.

When a comparison with Fig. 2b is made, can be denoted that the main negative contribution comes from the landfill and the base line scenarios in 11 out of 14 categories. Main negative contributions of scenario 1 and 2 against the landfill are maybe due to that the last corresponds to a sanitary landfill with high quality standards, that provide a safe place for final deposition. Positive contributions of the base line scenario are due to no fossil fuel consumption (except for transportation of the raw material for land application), avoided chemical fertilizer and discharges/emissions related. Nonetheless, the base line scenario has important negative contributions because the material with no treatment is spread over the field and produces emissions.

It is important to remark at this point, although a fixed nitrogen fertilizer replacement value was used to assess the bio-fertilizer and to calculate the chemical fertilizer avoided, the availability of N-com- pounds vary from substrate to substrate, thus scenario 1 and 2 produces a bio-fertilizer that is supposed to be of higher quality against the base line. However, no distinction was made tough, because an approximate
